title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: validate_join() analyzes potential join without performing , reporting match rates, relationship type, duplicate keys, unmatched rows.
code: orders <- data.frame(   id     = c(1L, 2L, 3L, 3L, 4L, 5L),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 175, 300, 50) ) customers <- data.frame(   id   = c(2L, 3L, 6L),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol") )  validate_join(orders, customers, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Validation: orders ↔ customers ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Keys in orders: id #> Keys in customers: id #>  #>   Item                                               Value #>   ───────────────────────────────────────────  ─────────── #>   Relationship                                 many-to-one #>   Key(s) in orders   [id]                          (1 col) #>   Key(s) in customers   [id]                       (1 col) #>   Rows in orders                                         6 #>   Distinct key combos in orders                          5 #>   Rows in customers                                      3 #>   Distinct key combos in customers                       3 #>   Overlapping distinct key combos                        2 #>   Matched row pairs (cartesian)                          3 #>   Match rate from orders                            50.00% #>   Match rate from customers                         66.67% #>   Rows only in orders (no match in customers)            3 #>   Rows only in customers (no match in orders)            1 #>  #> Duplicates: orders=yes customers=no

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: key columns different names, use named vector:
code: products <- data.frame(prod_id = 1:3, price = c(10, 20, 30)) sales    <- data.frame(item_id = c(1L, 1L, 2L), qty = c(5, 3, 7))  validate_join(products, sales, by = c("prod_id" = "item_id")) #>  #> ── Join Validation: products ↔ sales ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Keys in products: prod_id #> Keys in sales: item_id #>  #>   Item                                             Value #>   ─────────────────────────────────────────  ─────────── #>   Relationship                               one-to-many #>   Key(s) in products   [prod_id]                 (1 col) #>   Key(s) in sales   [item_id]                    (1 col) #>   Rows in products                                     3 #>   Distinct key combos in products                      3 #>   Rows in sales                                        3 #>   Distinct key combos in sales                         2 #>   Overlapping distinct key combos                      2 #>   Matched row pairs (cartesian)                        3 #>   Match rate from products                        66.67% #>   Match rate from sales                          100.00% #>   Rows only in products (no match in sales)            1 #>   Rows only in sales (no match in products)            0 #>  #> Duplicates: products=no sales=yes

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: Track impact numeric column stat (column name tables) stat_x/stat_y (different column names):
code: x <- data.frame(id = 1:4, revenue = c(100, 200, 300, 400)) y <- data.frame(id = c(2L, 3L, 5L), cost = c(10, 20, 30))  validate_join(x, y, by = "id", stat_x = "revenue", stat_y = "cost") #>  #> ── Join Validation: x ↔ y ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Keys in x: id #> Keys in y: id #>  #>   Item                                  Value #>   ───────────────────────────────  ────────── #>   Relationship                     one-to-one #>   Key(s) in x   [id]                  (1 col) #>   Key(s) in y   [id]                  (1 col) #>   Rows in x                                 4 #>   Distinct key combos in x                  4 #>   Rows in y                                 3 #>   Distinct key combos in y                  3 #>   Overlapping distinct key combos           2 #>   Matched row pairs (cartesian)             2 #>   Match rate from x                    50.00% #>   Match rate from y                    66.67% #>   Rows only in x (no match in y)            2 #>   Rows only in y (no match in x)            1 #>  #> ── Stat diagnostics ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> revenue in x: #> • Total: 1,000 #> • Matched: 500 (50.00%) #> • Unmatched: 500 (50.00%) #>  #> cost in y: #> • Total: 60 #> • Matched: 30 (50.00%) #> • Unmatched: 30 (50.00%) #>  #> Duplicates: x=no y=no

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: validate_primary_keys() tests whether set columns uniquely identify every row:
code: df <- data.frame(   id    = c(1L, 2L, 3L, 3L, 4L),   group = c("A", "A", "B", "C", "A"),   value = c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50) )  # Single column — not unique validate_primary_keys(df, "id") #>  #> ── Primary Key Validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Table: df #> Key column: id #>  #>   Metric                   Value #>   ───────────────────────  ───── #>   Total rows                   5 #>   Unique key combinations      4 #>   Duplicate key combos         1 #>  #> ✖ NO - Keys do NOT uniquely identify all rows. #>  #> Duplicate keys (showing up to 10): #>   id n #> 1  3 2  # Composite key — unique validate_primary_keys(df, c("id", "group")) #>  #> ── Primary Key Validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Table: df #> Key columns: id and group #>  #>   Metric                   Value #>   ───────────────────────  ───── #>   Total rows                   5 #>   Unique key combinations      5 #>   Duplicate key combos         0 #>  #> ✔ YES - Keys uniquely identify all rows.

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: validate_var_relationship() determines relationship two columns:
code: df2 <- data.frame(   dept    = c("Sales", "Sales", "Engineering", "Engineering"),   manager = c("Ann", "Ann", "Bob", "Bob") ) validate_var_relationship(df2, "dept", "manager") #>  #> ── Variable Relationship Validation ──────────────────────────────────────────── #> Table: df2 #> Variables: dept ↔ manager #>  #>   Metric                        Value #>   ────────────────────────────  ───── #>   Unique values in dept             2 #>   Unique values in manager          2 #>   Unique (dept, manager) pairs      2 #>  #> dept → manager: one-to-one #> manager → dept: one-to-one #>  #> Relationship: ONE-TO-ONE

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: compare_tables() compares two data.frames examining columns, row counts, key overlap, numeric discrepancies:
code: before <- data.frame(id = 1:5, value = c(10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0)) after  <- data.frame(id = 1:5, value = c(10.0, 22.5, 30.0, 40.0, 55.0))  compare_tables(before, after) #>  #> ── Table Comparison: before vs after ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> 1. Row counts #> before: 5 rows #> after: 5 rows #> Difference: = #>  #> 2. Column names #> Matching columns: 2 #> Only in before: 0 #> Only in after: 0 #> Type mismatches: 0 #>  #> 3. Row matching #> Only in before: 0 #> Only in after: 0 #> Matched, no discrepancies: 3 (60%) #> Matched, with discrepancies: 2 (40%) #> Total cell discrepancies: 2 (2 numeric, 0 categorical) #>  #> 5. title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: filter_keep() filter_drop() filter data printing diagnostics removed.

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: Keeps rows condition TRUE (dplyr::filter()):
code: sales <- data.frame(   id     = 1:10,   amount = c(500, 25, 1200, 80, 3000, 15, 750, 40, 2000, 60),   status = rep(c("valid", "suspect"), 5) )  result <- filter_keep(sales, amount > 100, .stat = amount) #> filter_keep(sales, amount > 100) #> Dropped 5 of 10 rows (50.00%). #> Dropped 220 of 7,670 for amount (2.87%).

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: Drops rows condition TRUE (inverse):
code: result2 <- filter_drop(sales, status == "suspect", .stat = amount) #> filter_drop(sales, status == "suspect") #> Dropped 5 of 10 rows (50.00%). #> Dropped 220 of 7,670 for amount (2.87%).

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: Set .warn_threshold get warning many rows dropped:
code: filter_keep(sales, amount > 1000, .stat = amount, .warn_threshold = 0.5) #> filter_keep(sales, amount > 1000) #> Dropped 7 of 10 rows (70.00%). #> Dropped 1,470 of 7,670 for amount (19.17%). #> Warning: Dropped 70.0% of rows exceeds threshold (50.0%). #>   id amount status #> 1  3   1200  valid #> 2  5   3000  valid #> 3  9   2000  valid

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: diagnose_nas() reports NA counts percentages every column:
code: messy <- data.frame(   id    = 1:6,   name  = c("A", NA, "C", "D", NA, "F"),   score = c(10, 20, NA, NA, 50, NA),   grade = c("A", "B", "C", NA, "A", "B") )  diagnose_nas(messy) #>  #> ── Missing Value Diagnosis ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> 3 of 4 columns have missing values #>  #>   Variable  N NA  Pct NA #>   ────────  ────  ────── #>   score        3   50.0% #>   name         2   33.3% #>   grade        1   16.7%

text: diagnose_strings() audits character vector common quality issues:
code: firms <- c("Apple", "APPLE", "apple", "  Microsoft ", "Google", NA, "") diagnose_strings(firms) #>  #> ── String Column Diagnosis: firms ────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Total elements: 7 #>  #> Missing & Empty: #> • NA values: 1 (14.3%) #> • Empty strings: 1 (14.3%) #> • Whitespace-only: 0 (0.0%) #>  #> Whitespace Issues: #> • Leading whitespace: 1 #> • Trailing whitespace: 1 #>  #> Encoding: #> • Non-ASCII chars: 0 #>  #> Case Inconsistencies: #> • Variant groups: 1 #> • Total variants: 3 #>  #> Case variant examples (up to 5 groups): #>  lower n_variants            examples #>  apple          3 Apple, APPLE, apple

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
text: audit_transform() shows exactly transformation function changes. automatically detects vector type computes type-appropriate diagnostics: Numeric: mean/min/max shift, proportion changed Date/POSIXct: range change, proportion shifted Factor: level remapping (gained/lost levels) Logical: TRUE/FALSE/NA balance shift Character: string-level /comparison

title: Diagnostic Functions Guide
code: audit_transform(firms, trimws) #>  #> ── Transformation Audit [character]: firms ───────────────────────────────────── #> Function: trimws #>  #>   Metric                       Value #>   ──────────────  ────────────────── #>   Total elements                   7 #>   NA (before)                      1 #>   NA (after)                       1 #>   Changed         1 (14.3% of total) #>   Unchanged                        6 #>  #> Examples of changes (showing 1 of 1): #>        before     after #>    Microsoft  Microsoft #>  #> Access cleaned vector with: `result$cleaned` audit_transform(firms, tolower) #>  #> ── Transformation Audit [character]: firms ───────────────────────────────────── #> Function: tolower #>  #>   Metric                       Value #>   ──────────────  ────────────────── #>   Total elements                   7 #>   NA (before)                      1 #>   NA (after)                       1 #>   Changed         4 (57.1% of total) #>   Unchanged                        3 #>  #> Examples of changes (showing 4 of 4): #>        before        after #>         Apple        apple #>         APPLE        apple #>    Microsoft    microsoft  #>        Google       google #>  #> Access cleaned vector with: `result$cleaned`

code: prices <- c(10.456, 20.789, 30.123, NA, 50.999) audit_transform(prices, round) #>  #> ── Transformation Audit [numeric]: prices ────────────────────────────────────── #> Function: round #>  #>   Metric                       Value #>   ──────────────  ────────────────── #>   Total elements                   5 #>   NA (before)                      1 #>   NA (after)                       1 #>   Changed         4 (80.0% of total) #>   Unchanged                        1 #>  #> Numeric summary: #>   Metric  Before  After #>   ──────  ──────  ───── #>   Mean     28.09     28 #>   SD       17.26  17.38 #>   Min      10.46     10 #>   Median   25.46   25.5 #>   Max         51     51 #>   NaN          0      0 #>   Inf          0      0 #>  #> Mean absolute delta: 0.1977 #> Changed beyond tolerance: 4 (100.0%) #>  #> Examples of changes (showing 4 of 4): #>  before after #>  10.456    10 #>  20.789    21 #>  30.123    30 #>  50.999    51 #>  #> Access cleaned vector with: `result$cleaned`

code: dates <- as.Date(c("2024-01-15", "2024-06-30", "2024-12-01", NA)) audit_transform(dates, function(d) d + 30) #>  #> ── Transformation Audit [Date]: dates ────────────────────────────────────────── #> Function: function(d) d + 30 #>  #>   Metric                       Value #>   ──────────────  ────────────────── #>   Total elements                   4 #>   NA (before)                      1 #>   NA (after)                       1 #>   Changed         3 (75.0% of total) #>   Unchanged                        1 #>  #> Date range: #>   Metric           Before       After #>   ───────────  ──────────  ────────── #>   Min          2024-01-15  2024-02-14 #>   Max          2024-12-01  2024-12-31 #>   Span (days)       321.0       321.0 #>  #> Examples of changes (showing 3 of 3): #>      before      after #>  2024-01-15 2024-02-14 #>  2024-06-30 2024-07-30 #>  2024-12-01 2024-12-31 #>  #> Access cleaned vector with: `result$cleaned`

code: sizes <- factor(c("S", "M", "L", "XL", "XXL", "S", "M")) audit_transform(sizes, function(f) {   levels(f)[levels(f) %in% c("XL", "XXL")] <- "XL+"   f }) #>  #> ── Transformation Audit [factor]: sizes ──────────────────────────────────────── #> Function: function(f) {, levels(f)[levels(f) %in% c("XL", "XXL")] <- "XL+", f, #> and } #>  #>   Metric                       Value #>   ──────────────  ────────────────── #>   Total elements                   7 #>   NA (before)                      0 #>   NA (after)                       0 #>   Changed         2 (28.6% of total) #>   Unchanged                        5 #>  #> Factor levels: #> • Levels added: XL+ #> • Levels removed: XL, XXL #>  #> Level counts (before | after): #>   Level  Before  After #>   ─────  ──────  ───── #>   <NA>        0      0 #>   L           1      1 #>   M           2      2 #>   S           2      2 #>   XL          1      0 #>   XL+         0      2 #>   XXL         1      0 #>  #> Examples of changes (showing 2 of 2): #>  before after #>      XL   XL+ #>     XXL   XL+ #>  #> Access cleaned vector with: `result$cleaned`

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: building data pipelines, ’s easy lose track happens step. many rows dropped filter? join introduce duplicates? columns missing values now? tidyaudit’s audit trail captures metadata-snapshots step pipe — row counts, column counts, NA totals, numeric summaries — without storing data . gives lightweight, structured record pipeline’s behavior. trail object also allows custom functions increase flexibility capture domain-specific diagnostics.

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: Start creating trail object inserting audit_tap() calls pipeline. tap records snapshot passes data unchanged. Now print trail see snapshot timeline: timeline shows row counts, column counts, NA totals, change summaries consecutive steps. can see exactly many rows filter removed columns added.
code: # Sample data orders <- data.frame(   id       = 1:20,   customer = rep(c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dan", "Eve"), 4),   amount   = c(150, 200, 50, 300, 75, 120, 400, 90, 250, 60,                180, 210, 45, 320, 85, 130, 380, 95, 270, 55),   status   = rep(c("complete", "pending", "complete", "cancelled", "complete"), 4) )  trail <- audit_trail("order_pipeline")  result <- orders |>   audit_tap(trail, "raw") |>   filter(status == "complete") |>   audit_tap(trail, "complete_only") |>   mutate(tax = amount * 0.1) |>   audit_tap(trail, "with_tax") print(trail) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "order_pipeline" ─────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:25 #> Snapshots: 3 #>  #>   #  Label          Rows  Cols  NAs  Type #>   ─  ─────────────  ────  ────  ───  ──── #>   1  raw              20     4    0  tap  #>   2  complete_only    12     4    0  tap  #>   3  with_tax         12     5    0  tap #>  #> Changes: #>   From           To             Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ─────────────  ─────────────  ────  ────  ─── #>   raw            complete_only    -8     =    = #>   complete_only  with_tax          =    +1    =

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: Plain audit_tap() records data looks like, can’t tell changed. Operation-aware taps — left_join_tap(), filter_tap(), etc. — perform operation record enriched diagnostics.

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: Replace dplyr::left_join() + audit_tap() left_join_tap() capture match rates, relationship type, duplicate key information: Type column now shows join type, relationship, match rate — without leaving pipe. six dplyr join types supported: left_join_tap(), right_join_tap(), inner_join_tap(), full_join_tap(), anti_join_tap(), semi_join_tap().
code: customers <- data.frame(   customer = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dan"),   region   = c("East", "West", "East", "North") )  trail2 <- audit_trail("join_pipeline")  result2 <- orders |>   audit_tap(trail2, "raw") |>   left_join_tap(customers, by = "customer",                 .trail = trail2, .label = "with_region")  print(trail2) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "join_pipeline" ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:25 #> Snapshots: 2 #>  #>   #  Label        Rows  Cols  NAs  Type                                 #>   ─  ───────────  ────  ────  ───  ──────────────────────────────────── #>   1  raw            20     4    0  tap                                  #>   2  with_region    20     5    4  left_join (many-to-one, 80% matched) #>  #> Changes: #>   From  To           Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ────  ───────────  ────  ────  ─── #>   raw   with_region     =    +1   +4

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: filter_tap() keeps matching rows (like dplyr::filter()) recording many rows dropped: .stat argument tracks numeric column filter, reporting much total dropped — useful financial pipelines want know monetary impact filter. filter_out_tap() works way drops matching rows (inverse).
code: trail3 <- audit_trail("filter_pipeline")  result3 <- orders |>   audit_tap(trail3, "raw") |>   filter_tap(status == "complete",              .trail = trail3, .label = "complete_only") |>   filter_tap(amount > 100,              .trail = trail3, .label = "high_value",              .stat = amount) #> ℹ filter_tap: status == "complete" #> Dropped 8 of 20 rows (40.0%) #> ℹ filter_tap: amount > 100 #> Dropped 8 of 12 rows (66.7%) #> Stat amount: dropped 555 of 1,135  print(trail3) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "filter_pipeline" ────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:25 #> Snapshots: 3 #>  #>   #  Label          Rows  Cols  NAs  Type                           #>   ─  ─────────────  ────  ────  ───  ────────────────────────────── #>   1  raw              20     4    0  tap                            #>   2  complete_only    12     4    0  filter (dropped 8 rows, 40%)   #>   3  high_value        4     4    0  filter (dropped 8 rows, 66.7%) #>  #> Changes: #>   From           To             Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ─────────────  ─────────────  ────  ────  ─── #>   raw            complete_only    -8     =    = #>   complete_only  high_value       -8     =    =

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: Pass named list functions via .fns compute domain-specific diagnostics tap. function receives snapshot data argument (. formula lambdas) return value stored snapshot. print trail, custom results appear inline ↳ annotations directly snapshot row: rendering rules : Scalar return value → ↳ fn_name: value Named vector named list scalars → ↳ fn_name: key=val, key=val (truncated 60 characters) Complex object (data frame, unnamed vector, nested list) → ↳ fn_name: [complex — see audit_report()] suppress annotations display main table:
code: trail4 <- audit_trail("custom_example")  result4 <- orders |>   audit_tap(trail4, "raw", .fns = list(     n_complete   = ~sum(.$status == "complete"),          # scalar     amount_stats = ~c(mean = mean(.$amount),              # named vector                       max  = max(.$amount))   )) |>   filter(status == "complete") |>   audit_tap(trail4, "complete_only", .fns = list(     n_complete   = ~sum(.$status == "complete"),     amount_stats = ~c(mean = mean(.$amount),                       max  = max(.$amount))   )) print(trail4) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "custom_example" ─────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:26 #> Snapshots: 2 #>  #>   #  Label          Rows  Cols  NAs  Type #>   ─  ─────────────  ────  ────  ───  ──── #>   1  raw              20     4    0  tap  #>      ↳ n_complete: 12 #>      ↳ amount_stats: mean=173.25, max=400 #>   2  complete_only    12     4    0  tap  #>      ↳ n_complete: 12 #>      ↳ amount_stats: mean=94.58333, max=180 #>  #> Changes: #>   From  To             Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ────  ─────────────  ────  ────  ─── #>   raw   complete_only    -8     =    = print(trail4, show_custom = FALSE) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "custom_example" ─────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:26 #> Snapshots: 2 #>  #>   #  Label          Rows  Cols  NAs  Type #>   ─  ─────────────  ────  ────  ───  ──── #>   1  raw              20     4    0  tap  #>   2  complete_only    12     4    0  tap #>  #> Changes: #>   From  To             Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ────  ─────────────  ────  ────  ─── #>   raw   complete_only    -8     =    =

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: tap functions work without trail. .trail = NULL (default): diagnostic args: behaves like plain dplyr function .stat .warn_threshold: runs diagnostics prints results without recording trail makes easy add quick diagnostics pipeline without setting full trail.
code: # Plain filter — no diagnostics orders |> filter_tap(amount > 100) |> nrow() #> [1] 12  # Diagnostics without a trail orders |> filter_tap(amount > 100, .stat = amount) |> invisible() #> filter_keep(.data, amount > 100) #> Dropped 8 of 20 rows (40.00%). #> Dropped 555 of 3,465 for amount (16.02%).

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: wide datasets, computing numeric summaries every column unnecessary slows pipeline. Three parameters tap functions let control gets captured: .numeric_summary = FALSE — skips quantile computation entirely. .cols_include — character vector column names include snapshot’s schema (mutually exclusive .cols_exclude). .cols_exclude — character vector column names exclude. Core invariants — nrow, ncol, total_nas — always recorded regardless settings. “minimal” snapshot still knows data 54 columns 20 rows, schema describes three columns asked contains numeric summaries.
code: wide_data <- cbind(orders, matrix(rnorm(20 * 50), nrow = 20))  trail_ctrl <- audit_trail("snapshot_controls")  wide_data |>   audit_tap(trail_ctrl, "full_snapshot") |>   audit_tap(trail_ctrl, "minimal",             .numeric_summary = FALSE,             .cols_include = c("id", "amount", "status"))  print(trail_ctrl) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "snapshot_controls" ──────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:26 #> Snapshots: 2 #>  #>   #  Label          Rows  Cols  NAs  Type #>   ─  ─────────────  ────  ────  ───  ──── #>   1  full_snapshot    20    54    0  tap  #>   2  minimal          20    54    0  tap #>  #> Changes: #>   From           To       Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ─────────────  ───────  ────  ────  ─── #>   full_snapshot  minimal     =     =    =

title: Audit Trail Walkthrough
text: tab() produces one-way frequency tables two-way crosstabulations: tab_tap() embeds tabulation inside pipeline custom diagnostic annotation. runs tab() data, stores result snapshot, returns .data unchanged — transparent pass-. useful tracking distributions shift across pipeline steps.
code: tab(orders, status) #>  #> ── Tabulation: status ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> 20 observations #>  #>   Value      Freq  Percent    Cum. #>   ─────────  ────  ───────  ────── #>   cancelled     4    20.0%   20.0% #>   complete     12    60.0%   80.0% #>   pending       4    20.0%  100.0% #>   ─────────  ────  ───────  ────── #>   Total        20   100.0% tab(orders, status, customer) #>  #> ── Crosstabulation: status × customer ────────────────────────────────────────── #> 20 observations | Cell contents: count #>  #>   status     Alice  Bob  Carol  Dan  Eve  Total #>   ─────────  ─────  ───  ─────  ───  ───  ───── #>   cancelled      0    0      0    4    0      4 #>   complete       4    0      4    0    4     12 #>   pending        0    4      0    0    0      4 #>   ─────────  ─────  ───  ─────  ───  ───  ───── #>   Total          4    4      4    4    4     20 trail_tab <- audit_trail("tab_pipeline")  result_tab <- orders |>   tab_tap(status, .trail = trail_tab, .label = "status_dist") |>   filter(status == "complete") |>   tab_tap(customer, .trail = trail_tab, .label = "customer_dist",           .sort = "freq_desc")  print(trail_tab) #>  #> ── Audit Trail: "tab_pipeline" ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-03-23 13:32:26 #> Snapshots: 2 #>  #>   #  Label          Rows  Cols  NAs  Type #>   ─  ─────────────  ────  ────  ───  ──── #>   1  status_dist      20     4    0  tap  #>      ↳ tab(status): [complex — see audit_report()] #>   2  customer_dist    12     4    0  tap  #>      ↳ tab(customer): [complex — see audit_report()] #>  #> Changes: #>   From         To             Rows  Cols  NAs #>   ───────────  ─────────────  ────  ────  ─── #>   status_dist  customer_dist    -8     =    =

title: Pipeline Audit Trails and Data Diagnostics for tidyverse Workflows
code: library(tidyaudit) library(dplyr) set.seed(123)  orders  <- data.frame(id = 1:100, amount = runif(100, 10, 500), region_id = sample(1:5, 100, TRUE)) regions <- data.frame(region_id = 1:4, name = c("North", "South", "East", "West"))  trail <- audit_trail("order_pipeline")  result <- orders |>   audit_tap(trail, "raw") |>   left_join_tap(regions, by = "region_id", .trail = trail, .label = "with_region") |>   filter_tap(amount > 100, .trail = trail, .label = "high_value", .stat = amount) #> ℹ filter_tap: amount > 100 #> Dropped 18 of 100 rows (18.0%) #> Stat amount: dropped 1,062.191 of 25,429.39  print(trail) #> ── Audit Trail: "order_pipeline" ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Created: 2026-02-21 14:36:35 #> Snapshots: 3 #>  #>   #  Label        Rows  Cols  NAs  Type                                 #>   ─  ───────────  ────  ────  ───  ──────────────────────────────────── #>   1  raw           100     3    0  tap                                  #>   2  with_region   100     4   23  left_join (many-to-one, 77% matched) #>   3  high_value     82     4   20  filter (dropped 18 rows, 18%)        #>  #> Changes: #>   raw → with_region: = rows, +1 cols, +23 NAs #>   with_region → high_value: -18 rows, = cols, -3 NAs  audit_diff(trail, "raw", "high_value") #> ── Audit Diff: "raw" → "high_value" ── #>  #>   Metric  Before  After  Delta #>   ──────  ──────  ─────  ───── #>   Rows       100     82    -18 #>   Cols         3      4     +1 #>   NAs          0     20    +20 #>  #> ✔ Columns added: name #>  #> Numeric shifts (common columns): #>     Column     Mean before  Mean after   Shift #>     ─────────  ───────────  ──────────  ────── #>     id               50.50       49.66   -0.84 #>     amount          254.29      297.16  +42.87 #>     region_id         3.08        3.05   -0.03

title: Pipeline Audit Trails and Data Diagnostics for tidyverse Workflows
text: Audit trail system — core innovation: audit_trail() / audit_tap() — build snapshot timelines inside pipes left_join_tap(), filter_tap(), friends — operation-aware taps enriched diagnostics (match rates, drop statistics) audit_diff() — detailed /comparison two snapshots audit_report() — full pipeline report one call audit_export() — self-contained HTML trail visualization (interactive flow diagram, server required) write_trail() / read_trail() — serialize trails RDS JSON CI pipelines dashboards tab_tap() — tabulation snapshots within pipelines (one-way two-way frequency tables) Snapshot controls (.numeric_summary, .cols_include, .cols_exclude) — fine-tune tap captures Diagnostic functions — tidyverse ports dtaudit: validate_join() — analyze joins without performing validate_primary_keys() / validate_var_relationship() — key validation compare_tables() — column, row, numeric comparison filter_keep() / filter_drop() — filter diagnostic output diagnose_nas() / summarize_column() / get_summary_table() — data quality diagnose_strings() / audit_transform() — string quality auditing transformation tab() — frequency tables crosstabulations sorting, cutoffs, weighting See audit trail walkthrough diagnostic functions guide detailed documentation.

text: x First data.frame tibble compare. y Second data.frame tibble compare. key_cols Character vector column names use keys matching rows. NULL (default), automatically detects character, factor, integer columns keys. tol Numeric tolerance comparing numeric columns. Differences less equal tol considered equal. Unmatched keys stored arbitrary order. compare_cols Character vector column names compare. NULL (default), common non-key columns compared. Mutually exclusive exclude_cols. exclude_cols Character vector column names exclude comparison. NULL (default), columns excluded. what: tidyaudit: Pipeline Audit Trails and Data Diagnostics for 'tidyverse' Workflows — tidyaudit-package
title: tidyaudit: Pipeline Audit Trails and Data Diagnostics for 'tidyverse' Workflows — tidyaudit-package
text: Provides pipeline audit trails data diagnostics 'tidyverse' workflows. audit trail system captures lightweight metadata snapshots step pipeline, building structured record without storing data . Operation-aware taps enrich snapshots join match rates filter drop statistics. Trails can serialized 'JSON' 'RDS' exported self-contained 'HTML' visualizations. Also includes diagnostic functions interactive data analysis including frequency tables, string quality auditing, data comparison.

title: tidyaudit: Pipeline Audit Trails and Data Diagnostics for 'tidyverse' Workflows — tidyaudit-package
text: Maintainer: Fernando Cordeiro fernandolpcordeiro@gmail.com [copyright holder]

Unnamed elements used tables. stat Optional single column name (string) track tables column name . title: Validate Join Operations Between Two Tables — validate_join
code: x <- data.frame(id = c(1L, 2L, 3L, 3L), value = c("a", "b", "c", "d")) y <- data.frame(id = c(2L, 3L, 4L), score = c(10, 20, 30)) result <- validate_join(x, y, by = "id") print(result) #>  #> ── Join Validation: x ↔ y ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Keys in x: id #> Keys in y: id #>  #>   Item                                   Value #>   ───────────────────────────────  ─────────── #>   Relationship                     many-to-one #>   Key(s) in x   [id]                   (1 col) #>   Key(s) in y   [id]                   (1 col) #>   Rows in x                                  4 #>   Distinct key combos in x                   3 #>   Rows in y                                  3 #>   Distinct key combos in y                   3 #>   Overlapping distinct key combos            2 #>   Matched row pairs (cartesian)              3 #>   Match rate from x                     75.00% #>   Match rate from y                     66.67% #>   Rows only in x (no match in y)             1 #>   Rows only in y (no match in x)             1 #>  #> Duplicates: x=yes y=no  # Track a stat column with different names in each table x2 <- data.frame(id = 1:3, sales = c(100, 200, 300)) y2 <- data.frame(id = 2:4, cost = c(10, 20, 30)) validate_join(x2, y2, by = "id", stat_x = "sales", stat_y = "cost") #>  #> ── Join Validation: x2 ↔ y2 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Keys in x2: id #> Keys in y2: id #>  #>   Item                                   Value #>   ────────────────────────────────  ────────── #>   Relationship                      one-to-one #>   Key(s) in x2   [id]                  (1 col) #>   Key(s) in y2   [id]                  (1 col) #>   Rows in x2                                 3 #>   Distinct key combos in x2                  3 #>   Rows in y2                                 3 #>   Distinct key combos in y2                  3 #>   Overlapping distinct key combos            2 #>   Matched row pairs (cartesian)              2 #>   Match rate from x2                    66.67% #>   Match rate from y2                    66.67% #>   Rows only in x2 (no match in y2)           1 #>   Rows only in y2 (no match in x2)           1 #>  #> ── Stat diagnostics ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> sales in x2: #> • Total: 600 #> • Matched: 500 (83.33%) #> • Unmatched: 100 (16.67%) #>  #> cost in y2: #> • Total: 60 #> • Matched: 30 (50.00%) #> • Unmatched: 30 (50.00%) #>  #> Duplicates: x2=no y2=no

title: tidyaudit 0.2.0
text: audit_export() — self-contained HTML trail visualization (interactive pipeline flow diagram, light/dark theme, clickable nodes/edges). server, Shiny required. Serialization functions: trail_to_list(), trail_to_df(), write_trail(), read_trail() machine-readable trail export (RDS JSON formats). tab() / tab_tap() — frequency tables crosstabulations within pipelines. One-way two-way tables .sort, .cutoff, .wt, .na, .display options. Snapshot controls: .numeric_summary, .cols_include / .cols_exclude parameters taps limit snapshot scope wide datasets. print.audit_trail() improvements: tabular “Changes” block //Rows/Cols/NAs columns +N/-N/= deltas; inline custom diagnostic annotations (.fns results) snapshot row show_custom parameter.

title: tidyaudit 0.2.0
text: audit_tap(): parameter label renamed .label API consistency taps (deprecation — early-stage project). Join taps: .stat now accepts bare column names (NSE via enquo()/as_label()), matching filter tap behavior. Quoted strings still work. Internal snapshot field col_info renamed schema (internal , user-facing API change).

title: tidyaudit 0.2.0
text: audit_transform() now generic across vector types (numeric, Date/POSIXct, factor, logical, character) type-appropriate diagnostics. audit_report(): removed unimplemented format = "rmd" option (deferred future version).
